steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants a specific example of onomatopoeia — meaning a concrete word that imitates the sound it represents. General descriptions of patterns or definitions don't qualify; the choice must name an actual onomatopoeic word and show it in action.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The languages of Mandarin Chinese and Malay use different words to represent the sound of a heartbeat.': Describes a pattern (variation across languages) without naming any specific onomatopoeic word. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Differences in how languages are structured can cause variations in the words used in different languages to represent the same sound.': A general explanation of why variation exists — no specific word given at all. ✗
  • Choice C — 'The term "onomatopoeia," which refers to words that imitate the sound they represent, combines the Greek words for "name" and "to make."': Defines and gives the etymology of the term itself, but provides no example of an actual onomatopoeic word. ✗
  • Choice D — 'The Malay word "dup dap dup dap" both imitates and represents the sound of a heartbeat.': Names a specific word and explicitly shows how it imitates a real sound — a direct, concrete example of onomatopoeia. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
